narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad years and that solid LSAT score of 35—it must feel like the ground shifted under you. Our prediction model looked at patterns from thousands of students just like you, and it estimated a 71.8% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam, which is why we had to make this tough call on your application. The biggest red flag for it was your Year 1 grades, landing you in the 5th decile at school—that early stumble pulled hardest toward this outcome, as it often signals challenges in ramping up to law's demands. Then came your Year 3 decile of 3, reinforcing that sense of not quite finding your stride even later on, making it the second strongest push toward failure. Your upper-middle family income, while a good foundation, surprisingly nudged things in the same direction in the model's eyes, perhaps because it spotted patterns where that background didn't always translate to the grit needed for bar success. Layered on top were your undergraduate GPA of 3.4 and that LSAT, both respectable but tilting further toward risk based on how similar profiles have fared. Your white racial background and full-time study plans added to that cautionary picture, even as your gender as a female offered a slight counterbalance, hinting at strengths others have shown in passing the bar. It's this combination, like pieces of a puzzle forming a familiar story of early hurdles persisting, that painted the full picture for us. You're clearly capable—full-time commitment shows real dedication—and with targeted steps like bolstering those foundational habits from Year 1, you could rewrite this story. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong; law school might just need a different entry point for now. Keep pushing; your potential shines through.
